verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is a canonical non-histone nuclear protein (primary compartment) that lacks a TM domain, GPI anchor, or classical signal peptide. Under baseline conditions it resides in the nucleus with continuous nucleocytoplasmic shuttling. However, published lactoperoxidase cell-surface iodination and affinity-purified antibody staining on intact, non-permeabilized cells document an outer-leaflet pool under secretion-stimulating or cell-death conditions. During immune activation, ICD, apoptosis, necrosis, and pyroptosis, HMGB1 translocates nucleus→cytoplasm→extracellular space as a soluble DAMP/cytokine (signaling via RAGE, TLR2, TLR4 on other cells). The outer-leaflet pool is real but context-dependent (activation/stress/cell-death states), not a stable wash-resistant anchored display. Pre-no checklist: no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering; no narrow germline/developmental surface display with own anchor; no lysosomal-TM-resident biology (lysosomal exocytosis is a release mechanism, endpoint is soluble); no stable PM minority pool at baseline; cell-death/activation-induced outer-leaflet exposure is documented gene-specifically. Verdict: contextual / cell_state_induced.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The outer-leaflet surface pool (lactoperoxidase iodination data) may reflect transient heparan-sulfate or electrostatic association that is wash-sensitive rather than true stable surface anchoring; no wash-resistance experiments for this pool are published for HMGB1 specifically.
